---
title: "Week 08 — Thursday Workshop: Project Progress, Peer Review & Method Justification"
format: html
---

Week: 8

Topic: Project Progress, Peer Review & Method Justification

| Part | Activity | Time |
|------|----------|------|
| 1 | Weekly Self-Assessment | 10 min |
| 2 | Start / Extend Your Methods Section | 10 min |
| 3 | Peer Feedback | ~15 min |
| 4 | Method Justification — Written Exercise | 15 min |
| 5 | Assumption Check | 10 min |
| 6 | Reflective Activity | 10 min |
| 7 | Prepare for Next Week's Presentation | 10 min |
| 8 | Project Work Time | remaining |

---

## Part 1: Weekly Self-Assessment (10 min)

Open the [Weekly Self-Assessment](../projects/weekly-self-assessment.html) page and work through it now.

The short version:

- Open your project README and find your **Expected Timeline**.
- Reflect on what you planned to do last week and what you actually did.
- Pick your status: 🟢 On track · 🟡 Slightly behind · 🔴 Significantly behind
- If 🟡 or 🔴: write one concrete step you will take this week to catch up.
- Identify **one specific thing you will finish by the end of this week**.
- Share your status and your one goal with the person next to you.

::: {.callout-tip}
## Update your README now if you haven't already

Go to your GitHub repository and make sure your Expected Timeline is there. It doesn't need to be perfect — it needs to exist and be honest about where you are.
:::

---

## Part 2: Start Working on Your Methods (10 min)

In your project (RStudio, GitHub, or Word) open or create a **Methods** section. Write at least two sentences:

- *I chose X methods because ...*
- *A key limitation of my analysis is ... so we ...*

If you already have this section, extend it. Add one more sentence about your data structure or your response variable distribution.

---

## Part 3: Peer Feedback — We will do this most weeks (15 min)

Pair with someone **outside** your immediate research area.

**⏱ ~7 minutes per person**

| Time | Task |
|------|------|
| 4 min | Explain your project: question, data, and why it matters |
| 1 min | Show your current work: code, figures, or written sections |
| 2 min | Receive feedback from your reviewer |

### Reviewer Feedback Guide

::: {.callout-note}
## Peer Feedback Guide

**Strengths** (at least one):
> "One thing that is working well is..."

**One Idea**:
> "One thing you might consider is..."

**One Hard Question**:
> Ask one question the presenter may not have considered yet.

:::

### After Both Rounds

Write down:

1. **One piece of feedback I received that I will act on:**

<br><br>

2. **One idea I gave my partner that also applies to my own work:**

<br><br>

---

## Part 4: Method Justification — Written Exercise (15 min)

In your project repository, write or expand your **Method Justification** section.

Your paragraph should address:

::: {.callout-tip}
## Writing Checklist

⬜ What is your response variable and what distribution does it follow?  
⬜ What is your data structure (independent, nested, repeated, blocked)?  
⬜ Why is a simpler analysis insufficient?  
⬜ Why is a more complex analysis not warranted?  
⬜ What assumptions does your method make? List ALL OF THEM!
:::

---

## Part 5: Check Your Assumptions (10 min)

List every assumption your chosen model requires. Then rate each one:

| Assumption | Status |
|------------|--------|
| *(e.g., independence of residuals)* | 🟢 Checked: and it holds |
| *(e.g., normality of random effects)* | 🟡 Haven't checked yet: but I could by next class |
| *(e.g., no spatial autocorrelation)* | 🔴 Can't fully check: need to acknowledge it and discuss it |

**The goal is not to have all green.** Red assumptions are honest, and they belong in your discussion section.

---

## Part 6: Reflective Activity — Learning Check-In (10 min)

Take 5 minutes on your own, then 5 minutes to share with a neighbor.

::: {.callout-note}
## Reflection Prompts

Think about your experience working on this project so far — not just the results, but the *process*.

**1. What is one thing about quantitative methods that feels more clear to you now than it did at the start of the semester?**

*(Be specific. "I understand statistics better" is not enough. Example: "I now understand why GLMM is appropriate for nested data, whereas before I would have just run an ANOVA.")*

<br><br>

**2. What is one thing you are still genuinely uncertain about in your own analysis?**

*(Not a vague worry — a specific methodological question you haven't resolved yet.)*

<br><br>

**3. What would you tell a student starting this course next year about how to approach the project?**

<br><br>

:::

These questions are for you. You don't hand them in. But writing them down — and saying them out loud — makes a difference.

---

## Part 7: Prepare for Next Week's Presentation (10 min)

**Next week during workshop time**, each student will give a **3–5 minute progress update**. This is informal, low-stakes, and does not require slides.

Read the [Progress Presentation Instructions](../projects/progress-presentation.html) now.

Then, use the remaining time to:

::: {.callout-important}
## Before You Leave Today

⬜ Update your Expected Timeline in your README (even if nothing changed — confirm it)  
⬜ Identify one specific thing you will show next week (a figure, table, output, or code chunk)  
⬜ Write down one open question you want feedback on from your classmates  
⬜ Push your latest work to GitHub  

:::

---

## Part 8: Project Work Time (remaining time)

Use the rest of the session to make progress on your project. Suggested priorities:

1. Run a preliminary model or analysis (even if rough)
2. Generate at least one exploratory figure
3. Continue writing your Methods section
4. Clean or organize your data file

Ask the instructor or a classmate if you get stuck. This is work time, not reading time.
